title: Rebasing and History
learningObjectives: Understand how rebase replays commits and rewrites SHA hashes to produce a linear history
Compare rebase vs merge and know when to use each
Use interactive rebase to squash, reword, and reorder commits
Apply the golden rule: never rebase commits that exist on a remote shared branch
Use --autosquash with fixup commits to automate squashing

theory.content:
What is Rebase?

Rebasing takes the commits from one branch and replays them on top of another branch, rewriting each commit's SHA hash in the process. The result is a perfectly linear history — as if development had always proceeded in a straight line.

By contrast, a merge preserves the full topology of when branches diverged and converged, creating a merge commit that records the joining of two histories.

# While on your feature branch, replay its commits onto main
git rebase main

After rebasing, your feature branch's commits appear as if they were written directly on top of the latest main commit. Each replayed commit gets a brand-new SHA hash.

Rebase vs Merge

AspectRebaseMerge
History shapeLinear — one straight lineNon-linear — branch topology preserved
New commits createdYes — replayed commits get new SHAsOnly a merge commit; originals unchanged
Conflict resolutionOnce per replayed commitOnce total, at the merge
Best forLocal cleanup before a PRIntegrating shared/public branches

The Golden Rule of Rebasing: Never rebase commits that have already been pushed to a remote shared branch. Rebase rewrites history; if other developers have based work on those commits, their histories will diverge and cause serious conflicts.

Interactive Rebase

Interactive rebase (git rebase -i HEAD~N) opens an editor showing the last N commits as a to-do list. You can rearrange lines and change the command for each commit:

  • pick — keep the commit as-is
  • reword — keep the commit but edit its message
  • edit — pause at this commit to amend it (add files, split it, etc.)
  • squash — combine this commit into the previous one; merge both messages
  • fixup — combine into the previous commit; discard this commit's message
  • drop — delete this commit entirely
  • exec — run a shell command after this commit
# Open interactive rebase for the last 4 commits
git rebase -i HEAD~4

# Sample rebase-todo file opened in editor:
pick a1b2c3 Add login form
pick d4e5f6 WIP: fix validation
pick 7g8h9i WIP: fix edge case
pick k0l1m2 Add tests for login

# Change 2nd and 3rd to squash:
pick a1b2c3 Add login form
squash d4e5f6 WIP: fix validation
squash 7g8h9i WIP: fix edge case
pick k0l1m2 Add tests for login

Workflow: Cleaning Up Before a PR

A common workflow is to squash "WIP" commits into logical, reviewable units before opening a pull request. You can automate this with --fixup commits and --autosquash:

# Create a fixup commit targeting a specific SHA
git commit --fixup a1b2c3

# Then run interactive rebase with autosquash — fixup commits are
# automatically reordered and marked as 'fixup' in the to-do list
git rebase -i --autosquash HEAD~N

This keeps your day-to-day commits flexible while ensuring a clean history in the final PR.
